context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string‚ÄĒpluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string‚Äôs sound. Our eyes see what‚Äôs happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 3

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 3


Problem 3.1 (20 pts)

Here we consider a double pendulum, each with one degree of freedom, as shown in Figure 1. Mass M1 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to a fixed origin. Its x-coordinate is X1 and it makes an angle őł1 with respect to the vertical (y-axis). Mass M2 is connected by a massless rigid rod of length L to mass M1. Its x-coordinate is X2 and it makes an angle őł2 with respect to the vertical direction.
You may assume that the 'hinges' at the origin and on mass M1 are frictionless. Gravity points downward in the y-direction. You may assume that all oscillations are small, i.e., that őł1 and őł2 are small and terms of order O(őł^2) are negligible.

[Figure 1: a double pendulum ‚ÄĒ rod of length L from the origin to M1 (angle őł1), and a second rod of length L from M1 to M2 (angle őł2).]

(a) Write down the equations of motion for the two oscillating masses.
(b) Determine the two normal mode frequencies of oscillation in the small amplitude limit as a function of ŌČ0 and őĪ, where ŌČ0^2 = g/L and őĪ = M2/M1. (You don't have to solve the corresponding amplitude ratios.)
(c) What are the two normal mode frequencies if őĪ ‚Üí ‚ąě? Do your results make sense?
(d) What are the two normal mode frequencies if őĪ ‚Üí 0? Do your results make sense?


Problem 3.2 (20 pts)

Consider two identical LC circuits as shown in Figure 2. The two inductors are brought close together such that their mutual inductance M results in a coupling between the currents flowing in the two circuits.

[Figure 2: two identical LC circuits (each with capacitor C and inductor L) side by side, coupled through the mutual inductance M between the two inductors.]

(a) Find the frequencies of normal modes as a function of given parameters.
(b) What current patterns correspond to these normal modes? Could you use symmetry arguments to discover these modes?


Problem 3.3 (20 pts)

Consider three identical masses constrained to move on a frictionless circle. The masses are connected with identical springs each with spring constant k (see Figure 3). The circle is large such that you can ignore any effects related to the curvature. The circle is horizontal such that gravity can be ignored.

[Figure 3: three masses m equally spaced on a circle, each connected to its neighbors by springs of constant k.]

(a) Find equations of motion for the three masses in terms of the small displacements from the equilibrium position of each mass.
(b) Determine the frequencies and the relative amplitudes for each of the normal modes. Make a simple sketch of the motion of the masses for each of the normal modes. How many different frequencies are there in this system?
(c) Because the masses are connected in a circle, some of the results of the normal mode calculations do not correspond to oscillatory motion. Explain why.


Problem 3.4 (20 pts)

Consider two identical masses m connected together with a spring and attached with another spring to a moving support (see Figure 4). The support is oscillating vertically and its position is given by h(t) = A¬∑cos(ŌČt). The Hooke constant of the two identical springs is k. Ignore effects of damping.

[Figure 4: an oscillating support at the top moving as h(t); below it a spring k to mass m (position y1(t)), then a spring k to mass m (position y2(t)).]

(a) Find coupled differential equations that govern displacements from equilibrium of the masses y1(t) and y2(t). Express your results in terms of ŌČ0^2 = k/m. Note that the effect of gravity results in a shift of the equilibrium position but does not affect the harmonic motion.
(b) Find the steady state response of the positions of the two masses y1(t) and y2(t). Make a careful sketch of the amplitude as a function of the driving frequency ŌČ for each of the masses.
(c) By inspecting the results of (b), give the frequencies and amplitude ratios for the normal modes of the undriven system.


Problem 3.5 (20 pts)

Two identical beads, each of mass m, are equally spaced along a massless string of length 3a (see Figure 5). Consider the system to be on a frictionless horizontal surface. Initially both ends of the string are fixed (őĒ = 0). Assume that the string is under tension T at all times. Beads can execute small amplitude oscillations perpendicular to the string (displacements are exaggerated in the figure).

[Figure 5: two beads m on a string of length 3a, divided into three segments of length a; left end fixed to a wall, right end attached to a support that can be driven as Y(t) = őĒ¬∑cos(ŌČ_d¬∑t).]

(a) Find equations of motion for the two beads in terms of displacement from the equilibrium y1(t), y2(t).
(b) Find and sketch the motion of the normal modes and calculate the normal mode frequencies for the system.

Assume now that the rightmost attachment point undergoes harmonic oscillation y(t) = őĒ¬∑cos(ŌČ_d¬∑t).

(c) Find the steady state amplitude of the motion of the two masses as a function of the driving frequency ŌČ_d and the amplitude őĒ.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i‚Äôm on Problem 3.1 rn with the double pendulum and kinda stuck at the small-angle equations of motion. can u help me set those up without skipping straight to the final boxed answer?
exchanges.tutor: Good place to start! Before I jump in ‚ÄĒ what approach are you thinking of using to set up the equations of motion? (For example, Newton's second law directly, or the Lagrangian method?) And what have you written down so far?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help setting up the equations of motion for the double pendulum. I should use the Socratic method to guide them without giving anything away. The best first step is to ask them what they've tried so far and what approach they're planning to use (Lagrangian, Newtonian forces, etc.). This establishes their starting point and lets me tailor my guidance. I'll keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: tbh i was gonna use Lagrangian bc Newton‚Äôs law for the constraints gets messy. so far i‚Äôve got \(x_1 \approx L\theta_1\) and \(x_2 \approx L(\theta_1+\theta_2)\), but i‚Äôm not fully sure about the kinetic/potential energy terms.
exchanges.tutor: Your position expressions look correct ‚ÄĒ good start! Now let's build the Lagrangian piece by piece. Let's start simple: what is the kinetic energy of M1 alone, in terms of your coordinates?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a good start with the small-angle position expressions. I should affirm this and then guide them toward building the Lagrangian by thinking about kinetic and potential energy separately. Rather than telling them the expressions, I'll ask a focused question about one piece at a time ‚ÄĒ starting with kinetic energy for M1, which is the simpler one. This is guided discovery, bite-sized.

exchanges.student: i think for \(M_1\), since \(x_1 \approx L\theta_1\) and \(y_1\) barely changes to first order, it should be \(T_1=\tfrac12 M_1 L^2 \dot\theta_1^2\), right? also can u just help me write the full \(T\) for both masses so i don‚Äôt mess up the cross term lol
exchanges.tutor: T1 is exactly right! But I won't write T2 for you ‚ÄĒ that's your job. Here's a nudge: M2's position depends on *both* őł1 and őł2. So what is the velocity of M2, i.e., what is Šļč2 in terms of őł1 and őł2? Start there and the cross term will appear naturally.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got T1 correct and is now asking me to write the full kinetic energy for them. I must not do that ‚ÄĒ that would be completing the task for the student. Instead, I should affirm T1 and then ask them to think about T2 on their own, specifically prompting them to think about the velocity of M2 in terms of the coordinates. The cross term will emerge naturally if they think carefully about the velocity of M2.

exchanges.student: gotcha, so \(x_2 \approx L(\theta_1+\theta_2)\) gives \(\dot x_2 \approx L(\dot\theta_1+\dot\theta_2)\). since \(y_2\) also only changes at higher order for small angles, i‚Äôm guessing \(T_2=\tfrac12 M_2 L^2(\dot\theta_1+\dot\theta_2)^2\) ‚ÄĒ is that the right move?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right! So now you have both T1 and T2. What about the potential energy? Think about how the height of each mass changes with the angles ‚ÄĒ what are V1 and V2 in the small-angle limit?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly derived T2. Now I should affirm this and prompt them to move on to the potential energy, keeping the momentum going. I'll ask them about V next, keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: yeah i think \(V_1 \approx \tfrac12 M_1 gL\,\theta_1^2\). for \(M_2\), since it picks up height change from both rods, is it \(V_2 \approx \tfrac12 M_2 gL(\theta_1^2+\theta_2^2)\) or am i missing a mixed term? ngl if there‚Äôs a clean way to write the full \(L=T-V\) in matrix form, that‚Äôd help a ton.
exchanges.tutor: Good news ‚ÄĒ your V2 is actually correct! The height of M2 picks up independent contributions from each rod. Now, rather than jumping to matrix form, try writing out the Euler-Lagrange equation for őł1 first: what do you get for d/dt(‚ąāL/‚ąāőłŐá1) - ‚ąāL/‚ąāőł1 = 0?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student got V1 right but has an error in V2 ‚ÄĒ the height of M2 depends on both angles independently (it's the sum of the two height changes, not a product), so actually V2 = M2*g*L*(1-cosőł1) + M2*g*L*(1-cosőł2) ‚Čą (1/2)M2*g*L*(őł1^2 + őł2^2), which is actually what they wrote. Wait ‚ÄĒ let me reconsider. The height of M2 relative to the origin: y2 = -L*cosőł1 - L*cosőł2, so the potential energy is V2 = -M2*g*(L*cosőł1 + L*cosőł2) ‚Čą M2*g*L*(őł1^2/2 + őł2^2/2). So their V2 is actually correct! I should confirm this. As for writing the full Lagrangian in matrix form ‚ÄĒ I should not do that for them. I'll affirm V2 and ask them to write the Euler-Lagrange equations themselves, which is the next step toward the equations of motion.

exchanges.student: i get the idea, but i‚Äôm kinda pressed for time rn ‚ÄĒ can u just write the two linearized EOMs explicitly so i can check mine fast? i‚Äôll do the normal-mode algebra myself after that.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you on the time pressure, but giving you the equations directly isn't something I can do ‚ÄĒ that's your work to produce. Share what you've derived so far for the two Euler-Lagrange equations, even if incomplete, and I'll help you check your reasoning step by step. That'll actually be faster than starting from scratch.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the answer directly, which I must refuse. I should firmly but kindly remind them of my role and redirect them to show me what they have so far, so I can help them check their work through guided discovery.

exchanges.student:
ok fair ‚ÄĒ i think i got for \(\theta_1\):
\[
(M_1+M_2)L^2\ddot\theta_1 + M_2L^2\ddot\theta_2 + (M_1+M_2)gL\,\theta_1 = 0
\]
and for \(\theta_2\):
\[
M_2L^2\ddot\theta_1 + M_2L^2\ddot\theta_2 + M_2gL\,\theta_2 = 0
\]
can u check if that‚Äôs right or if i dropped some term?
exchanges.tutor: Both equations are correct ‚ÄĒ well done! Now for part (b), to find the normal mode frequencies you need to assume a trial solution of the form őł1, őł2 ~ e^(iŌČt). What does that substitution turn your equations of motion into?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has derived both equations of motion. Let me check them carefully. For the Lagrangian: T = (1/2)M1*L^2*őłŐá1^2 + (1/2)M2*L^2*(őłŐá1+őłŐá2)^2, V = (1/2)M1*g*L*őł1^2 + (1/2)M2*g*L*(őł1^2+őł2^2). For őł1: ‚ąāL/‚ąāőłŐá1 = M1*L^2*őłŐá1 + M2*L^2*(őłŐá1+őłŐá2) = (M1+M2)*L^2*őłŐá1 + M2*L^2*őłŐá2. d/dt of this = (M1+M2)*L^2*őłŐą1 + M2*L^2*őłŐą2. ‚ąāL/‚ąāőł1 = -(M1+M2)*g*L*őł1. So EOM1: (M1+M2)*L^2*őłŐą1 + M2*L^2*őłŐą2 + (M1+M2)*g*L*őł1 = 0. This matches the student's first equation. For őł2: ‚ąāL/‚ąāőłŐá2 = M2*L^2*(őłŐá1+őłŐá2). d/dt = M2*L^2*(őłŐą1+őłŐą2). ‚ąāL/‚ąāőł2 = -M2*g*L*őł2. EOM2: M2*L^2*(őłŐą1+őłŐą2) + M2*g*L*őł2 = 0, which simplifies to M2*L^2*őłŐą1 + M2*L^2*őłŐą2 + M2*g*L*őł2 = 0. This also matches the student's second equation. Both equations are correct! I should affirm this and push them toward part (b) ‚ÄĒ finding the normal mode frequencies.

exchanges.student: nice, thx. after substituting \( \theta_j=A_j e^{i\omega t}\), i get a 2x2 system for \(A_1,A_2\). can u just give me the determinant equation in terms of \(\omega_0^2=g/L\) and \(\alpha=M_2/M_1\)? i just wanna compare my setup before i grind the algebra.
exchanges.tutor: Still can't hand that over ‚ÄĒ but you're close! Write out the 2x2 system you get for A1 and A2 after the substitution, and we can check whether it's set up correctly before you take the determinant. What do you have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to provide the answer directly. I must refuse and redirect them to show their work. I should ask them to write out the 2x2 system they got after substitution, and then they can derive the determinant condition themselves. I'll keep it firm but encouraging.

exchanges.student:
ugh ok, here‚Äôs what i have after dividing by \(L^2\) and using \(\omega_0^2=g/L\), \(\alpha=M_2/M_1\):
\[
\big[(1+\alpha)(\omega_0^2-\omega^2)\big]A_1-\alpha \omega^2 A_2=0
\]
\[
-\omega^2 A_1+(\omega_0^2-\omega^2)A_2=0
\]
is that setup right, or did i screw a sign/factor somewhere?
exchanges.tutor: Both rows are correct ‚ÄĒ nice work getting here! Now the next step is yours: set the determinant of the coefficient matrix to zero for a non-trivial solution. What do you get when you expand it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Let me verify the student's 2x2 system carefully. From EOM1: (M1+M2)*L^2*(-ŌČ^2)*A1 + M2*L^2*(-ŌČ^2)*A2 + (M1+M2)*g*L*A1 = 0. Dividing by M1*L^2: (1+őĪ)(-ŌČ^2+ŌČ0^2)*A1 + őĪ*(-ŌČ^2)*A2 = 0, which gives [(1+őĪ)(ŌČ0^2-ŌČ^2)]*A1 - őĪ*ŌČ^2*A2 = 0. This matches the student's first equation. From EOM2: M2*L^2*(-ŌČ^2)*A1 + M2*L^2*(-ŌČ^2)*A2 + M2*g*L*A2 = 0. Dividing by M2*L^2: -ŌČ^2*A1 + (-ŌČ^2+ŌČ0^2)*A2 = 0, which gives -ŌČ^2*A1 + (ŌČ0^2-ŌČ^2)*A2 = 0. This matches the student's second equation. Both are correct! Now I should affirm and push them to take the determinant themselves.

exchanges.student: yeah that‚Äôs where i‚Äôm getting stuck tbh ‚ÄĒ when i expand it i keep getting messy terms. can u at least tell me if the determinant condition simplifies to a quadratic in \(x=\omega^2\)?
exchanges.tutor: Yes ‚ÄĒ it does reduce to a quadratic in ŌČ^2. So you're on the right track structurally. Show me your expanded determinant expression, even if it looks messy, and we'll identify exactly where things are going wrong.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to confirm whether the determinant simplifies to a quadratic in ŌČ^2. This is a small hint that doesn't give away the answer but validates their approach. I can confirm this since it's more of a structural hint about the form of the equation rather than the answer itself. Then I should push them to show me their expanded determinant so I can help them identify where they're getting stuck.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. No deductions were warranted based on the evidence in the transcript.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor consistently refused to hand over final answers or write out equations for the student (Turns 3, 6, 8, 10), instead redirecting the student to produce their own work and then checking it. Key idea generation remained student-led throughout: the student derived T1, T2, V1, V2, the EOMs, the matrix system, and the determinant setup themselves, with the tutor only confirming correctness and nudging the next step. Scaffolding was well-executed ‚ÄĒ the tutor built on each correct student contribution before advancing. Responses were concise and interactive, avoiding monologues. Tone was supportive and non-grading throughout. No off-topic drift or assignment misunderstanding occurred.
